tier_2_handbook.definition: Biliary tract cancers (BTCs) are a heterogeneous group of highly lethal malignancies arising from the epithelial lining of the biliary tree. This encompasses cancers originating from the gallbladder, intrahepatic bile ducts (within the liver parenchyma), and extrahepatic bile ducts (which include the perihilar/hilar and distal bile ducts). The biliary system is embryologically derived from the foregut endoderm. Anatomically, the biliary tree is divided into the intrahepatic and extrahepatic components. The gallbladder stores bile, while the intrahepatic bile ducts collect bile from the liver and merge to form the right and left hepatic ducts, which unite outside the liver to form the common hepatic duct. The cystic duct from the gallbladder joins the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct, which drains into the duodenum. BTCs are collectively defined as adenocarcinomas or other histologic types arising from this system. They are characterized by local invasion, vascular invasion, regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastases. Gallbladder cancer is the most common anatomic subtype, followed by extrahepatic CCA, which is more common than intrahepatic CCA [MS-2, MS-9].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: The vast majority of biliary tract cancers (over 90%) are adenocarcinomas. These are further classified by anatomic site and histologic growth patterns. Cholangiocarcinomas (CCAs) can be mass-forming (forming a distinct mass), periductal-infiltrating (growing along bile ducts), or intraductal-growing (growing within the bile duct lumen). Adenocarcinoma is the predominant histology for gallbladder cancer, intrahepatic CCA (iCCA), and extrahepatic CCA (eCCA), including perihilar (Klatskin) and distal subtypes.
subtypes.key_associations: This is the classic histologic type for which most staging, prognostic, and treatment data are derived.

subtypes.name: Adenosquamous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A subtype containing both glandular (adenocarcinoma) and squamous cell components.
subtypes.key_associations: Can occur in the gallbladder or bile ducts.

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.key_associations: May arise in the gallbladder or bile ducts.

subtypes.name: Small Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A neuroendocrine tumor type, distinct from the more common adenocarcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Should be staged and treated according to guidelines for neuroendocrine tumors if pure.

subtypes.name: Other Rare Histologies
subtypes.description: Includes lymphomas, sarcomas, and well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors.
subtypes.key_associations: For rare histologies with distinct systemic therapy options (e.g., pure neuroendocrine tumors or sarcomas), treatment should follow the relevant NCCN Guideline for that specific histologic type [GALL-B, INTRA-C, EXTRA-B].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis:
Biliary tract cancers harbor clinically relevant genomic alterations that differ by anatomic subsite. Tumor genomic profiling is recommended for patients with unresectable or metastatic BTCs. Key events include:
- **FGFR2 gene fusions/rearrangements**: Found in approximately 9%–15% of intrahepatic CCAs and are rare in other subsites. These lead to constitutive activation of FGFR signaling, promoting cell proliferation and tumorigenesis. Targetable by pemigatinib, futibatinib, and erdafitinib [BIL-B 5 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-29].
- **IDH1 mutations**: Occur in approximately 10%–20% of intrahepatic CCAs and are rare in other subsites. Mutations (most commonly at codon 132, R132X) lead to accumulation of the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate, impairing cellular differentiation. Targetable by ivosidenib [BIL-B 5 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-29].
- **BRAF V600E mutations**: Present in approximately 1%–5% of BTCs. This mutation results in constitutive activation of the MAPK signaling pathway. Targetable by dabrafenib/trametinib [BIL-B 4 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-28].
- **NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions**: Estimated to occur in <1% of BTCs. These fusions lead to expression of oncogenic TRK fusion proteins. Targetable by larotrectinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib [BIL-B 4 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-26].
- **MSI-H/dMMR**: Present in approximately 1%–3% of BTCs. Results from deficient DNA mismatch repair. Predicts response to immune checkpoint inhibitors like pembrolizumab [BIL-B 4 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-26].
- **TMB-H**: Found in <5% of advanced BTCs. Predicts response to immune checkpoint inhibitors [BIL-B 3 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-27].
- **HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification/activating mutations**: Present in approximately 5%–20% of CCAs and 15%–30% of gallbladder cancers. A member of the ErbB/EGFR family that activates downstream proliferative pathways. Targetable by trastuzumab deruxtecan, pertuzumab/trastuzumab, tucatinib/trastuzumab, and zanidatamab [BIL-B 6 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-30].
- **NRG1 gene fusions**: Found in <1% of BTCs, more commonly in iCCA and eCCA. Targetable by zenocutuzumab-zbco [BIL-B 6 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5].
- **KRAS G12C mutation**: Reported in about 1% of BTCs. Targetable by adagrasib [BIL-B 6 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-32].
- **RET gene fusions**: Rare (<1%). Targetable by pralsetinib and selpercatinib [BIL-B 6 of 8, BIL-C 3 of 5, MS-32].
Additionally, up to 10%–15% of BTCs may be associated with inherited cancer predisposition syndromes. Germline alterations in high- to moderate-penetrance genes like BRCA1/2, MLH1, MSH2, PALB2, and ATM have been identified in roughly 9%–11% of cases [BIL-B 1 of 8, MS-25].

epidemiology.incidence: Biliary tract cancers are relatively rare but highly lethal. In 2025, it is estimated that 42,240 people in the United States will be diagnosed with liver cancer including intrahepatic bile duct cancer, and an additional 12,610 people will be diagnosed with gallbladder cancer or other biliary tract cancer [MS-2]. Globally, there are pockets of increased incidence in Korea, Japan, some areas of Eastern Europe and South America (especially Bolivia, Chile, and Spain), and in females in India, Pakistan, and Ecuador [MS-3].
epidemiology.mortality: It is estimated that approximately 30,090 deaths from liver or intrahepatic bile duct cancer and 4,400 deaths due to gallbladder cancer or other biliary tract cancer will occur in the United States in 2025 [MS-2].
epidemiology.trends: From 1973 to 2015, the incidence of gallbladder cancer has decreased in both males and females, white individuals, and individuals of American Indian/Alaskan Native and Asian/Pacific Islander descent, but there was a small trend for an increase in Black individuals [MS-3]. Analyses of SEER data from 2001 to 2017 showed that the incidence of intrahepatic CCA increased dramatically (148.8%), while the incidence of extrahepatic CCA increased at a slower rate (7.5%). This rise in iCCA incidence may be partly due to improved diagnostic capabilities [MS-9, MS-10].
epidemiology.demographics: Incidence of gallbladder cancer steadily increases with age. Females are more likely to be diagnosed with gallbladder cancer than males. In the United States, incidence and mortality rates for gallbladder cancer are highest among individuals of American Indian, Alaska Native, and Asian/Pacific Islander descent [MS-3].

risk_factors.detail: The presence of chronic inflammation from gallstones is the most prevalent risk factor for gallbladder cancer. The risk increases with stone size [MS-3].

Calcification of the gallbladder wall due to chronic inflammation has been regarded as a risk factor, though subsequent reports suggest the risk (7%–15% cancer) is lower than historical estimates [MS-3].
Larger polyps are associated with a higher risk of malignancy. The Society of Radiologists in Ultrasound recommends surgical consultation for polyps ≥1.5 cm or ≥0.7 cm if indeterminate [MS-3].
A congenital anomaly that allows reflux of pancreatic juice into the biliary tree, causing chronic inflammation [MS-3].
A chronic cholestatic liver disease characterized by inflammation and fibrosis of the bile ducts, significantly increasing the risk of CCA [MS-9].
An association exists, which may be confounded by PSC, as PSC is common in IBD patients [MS-9].
Chronic gallbladder inflammation due to Salmonella typhi carrier state is a risk factor [MS-3].

risk_factors.factor: Hepatolithiasis (chronic calculi of the bile duct)
risk_factors.detail: Stones in the intrahepatic bile ducts cause chronic inflammation and are a well-established risk factor for CCA [MS-9].
Congenital dilations of the bile ducts are a well-established risk factor for CCA [MS-9].

risk_factors.factor: Liver fluke infections (Opisthorchis viverrini, Clonorchis sinensis)
risk_factors.detail: Parasitic infections endemic in Southeast Asia are well-established risk factors for CCA [MS-9].
A risk factor for intrahepatic CCA, similar to its role in hepatocellular carcinoma [MS-9].
A risk factor for intrahepatic CCA [MS-9].

risk_factors.type: Risk factor for iCCA and eCCA
risk_factors.detail: A systematic review showed association with increased incidence of both intrahepatic and extrahepatic CCA [MS-9].
Identified as a risk factor specific to intrahepatic CCA [MS-9].
Identified as a risk factor specific to intrahepatic CCA [MS-9].
Identified as a risk factor specific to intrahepatic CCA [MS-9].
Identified as a risk factor specific to intrahepatic CCA [MS-9].
A potential, albeit controversial, risk factor [MS-3].

tier_2_handbook.protective_factors: No protective factors for biliary tract cancers are explicitly mentioned in the provided source text.

gallbladder_cancer_clinical_features.typical_presentation: Gallbladder cancer often presents incidentally during cholecystectomy for presumed benign disease or on post-cholecystectomy pathologic review. It may also present as a suspicious mass on imaging, with jaundice, or with chronic right upper quadrant abdominal pain. The disease is commonly diagnosed at an advanced stage due to its aggressive nature and clinical mimicry of biliary colic or chronic cholecystitis [3-5].

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Presents with obstructive jaundice and is a poor prognostic indicator, associated with advanced stage (96% vs 60%; P<.001) and lower disease-specific survival (6 vs 16 months; P<.0001) compared to patients without jaundice [25,26].

symptoms.name: Right upper quadrant pain
symptoms.detail: Chronic pain may be the presenting symptom, often attributed to biliary colic or chronic cholecystitis [4].

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Nonspecific symptom associated with advanced malignancy [4].

signs.name: Palpable right upper quadrant mass
signs.detail: May be present in advanced cases [4].

signs.name: Hepatomegaly
signs.detail: May indicate liver invasion or metastasis [4].

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage disease is often asymptomatic and discovered incidentally [3-5].
Symptoms mimic benign gallbladder disease (biliary colic, chronic cholecystitis) leading to delayed diagnosis [4].
Jaundice may be absent until late stages [25].

gallbladder_cancer_red_flags: New-onset jaundice in the setting of gallbladder disease
Persistent right upper quadrant pain not responding to standard therapy
Incidental finding of gallbladder malignancy on pathologic review after cholecystectomy

diagnostic.test: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Essential for local staging, assessing tumor penetration, nodal involvement, and distant metastases [27].
diagnostic.key_findings: Evaluates tumor extent, vascular invasion, and adjacent organ involvement.

diagnostic.test: Chest CT with or without contrast

diagnostic.rationale: Often the initial imaging for right upper quadrant pain; may detect a mass or gallstones [3].
diagnostic.key_findings: May show gallbladder mass or wall thickening.

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred for evaluating biliary obstruction in jaundiced patients [27].
diagnostic.key_findings: Demonstrates biliary anatomy and obstruction level.

diagnostic.rationale: Used for therapeutic intervention (biliary stenting) rather than diagnosis [27].

staging.rationale: May identify occult peritoneal metastases, especially in locally advanced tumors (yield 25.2% vs 10.7% in early-stage; P=.02) [50].
staging.indication: Consider in patients with resectable disease to avoid non-therapeutic laparotomy.

biomarkers.rationale: Baseline tumor marker; not for diagnosis [27,33].
Baseline tumor marker; may be elevated in biliary obstruction or other cancers [27,33].
biomarkers.when: At initial workup, consider after biliary decompression

biomarkers.rationale: Assess hepatic reserve and biliary obstruction [4].

do_not_miss: Core biopsy is preferred over fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for tissue diagnosis [Footnote f, GALL-1].
Multidisciplinary evaluation by hepatobiliary surgeon, medical oncologist, and radiologist.
avoid: Avoid biopsy if clinical and radiologic suspicion is high and resection is planned, to prevent peritoneal dissemination [GALL-A].
Do not use CEA or CA 19-9 to confirm diagnosis; they are baseline tests only [p].

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed
No evidence of primary tumor
Carcinoma in situ
Tumor invades lamina propria or muscular layer
Tumor invades lamina propria
Tumor invades muscle layer

Tumor invades the perimuscular connective tissue on the peritoneal side, without involvement of the serosa (visceral peritoneum) OR tumor invades the perimuscular connective tissue on the hepatic side, with no extension into the liver
Tumor invades the perimuscular connective tissue on the peritoneal side, without involvement of the serosa (visceral peritoneum)
Tumor invades the perimuscular connective tissue on the hepatic side, with no extension into the liver

Tumor perforates the serosa (visceral peritoneum) and/or directly invades the liver and/or one other adjacent organ or structure, such as the stomach, duodenum, colon, pancreas, omentum, or extrahepatic bile ducts
Tumor invades main portal vein or hepatic artery or invades two or more extrahepatic organs or structures

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
Metastases to one to three regional lymph nodes
Metastases to four or more regional lymph nodes

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ, confined to epithelium.
Tumor invades lamina propria or muscle layer, no nodal or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: ~80-100% for T1a; T1b may have lower survival

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades peritoneal side perimuscular tissue, no nodal or distant metastasis.
Tumor invades hepatic side perimuscular tissue, no nodal or distant metastasis.
Tumor perforates serosa or invades adjacent organs, no nodal or distant metastasis.
Regional lymph node metastasis (1-3 nodes).
Main vessel invasion or multiple organ involvement, with or without regional nodes.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Often palliative; may consider neoadjuvant therapy

stage_groups.criteria: Any T N2 M0 or Any T Any N M1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Extensive nodal disease (≥4 nodes) or distant metastases.

staging_pearls: T2 is divided into T2a (peritoneal side) and T2b (hepatic side) due to differences in prognosis; hepatic side is associated with worse outcomes [19,20].
Regional lymph node staging is based on number of positive nodes (N1: 1-3, N2: ≥4) rather than anatomic location [19].
Histologic grade (G1-G3) is reported but not included in stage grouping [ST-1].

intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_clinical.typical_presentation: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) often presents as an isolated intrahepatic mass on imaging, with imaging characteristics consistent with malignancy but not typical for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Patients may be asymptomatic or present with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, weight loss, or abdominal pain. Symptoms of biliary obstruction are uncommon because these tumors do not necessarily involve the common hepatic/bile duct [INTRA-1, 85].

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, often vague.

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.detail: May be present, sometimes with cholangitis if biliary obstruction occurs.

signs.name: Jaundice
signs.detail: Uncommon at presentation unless centrally located or advanced.

atypical_or_missed: Often detected incidentally as an isolated liver mass on imaging for other reasons [85].
May be misdiagnosed as metastatic disease from another primary site.
Symptoms are nonspecific and may lead to delayed diagnosis.

intrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_red_flag: New liver mass on imaging, especially in setting of chronic liver disease or risk factors
Elevated liver function tests without clear cause
History of primary sclerosing cholangitis, hepatolithiasis, or liver fluke infection

diagnostic.test: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast (preferred) or contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS)
diagnostic.rationale: Essential for characterizing the mass, assessing vascular involvement, and staging [INTRA-1, 92-95].
diagnostic.key_findings: Heterogeneous enhancement, vascular invasion, satellite lesions.

diagnostic.test: Chest CT with or without contrast

diagnostic.rationale: Required for histologic diagnosis, especially if surgery is not immediately planned or for advanced disease [INTRA-1, INTRA-C].

staging.rationale: May identify occult peritoneal metastases; yield is higher in locally advanced tumors [50].
staging.indication: Consider in patients with potentially resectable disease.

biomarkers.rationale: Baseline tumor markers; not for diagnosis [INTRA-1, d].
Assess hepatic function and biliary obstruction [INTRA-1].
Consider to help differentiate from HCC, especially in chronic liver disease [INTRA-1].
biomarkers.when: Consider in patients with risk factors for HCC

do_not_miss: Consider esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy to rule out metastatic adenocarcinoma from gastrointestinal primary [INTRA-1].
Core biopsy is preferred over FNA for tissue diagnosis [INTRA-1, f].
Multidisciplinary evaluation including hepatologist, surgeon, and oncologist.
avoid: Avoid unnecessary biopsy if imaging is highly suggestive and resection is planned, to prevent seeding.
Do not rely solely on serum tumor markers for diagnosis.

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed
No evidence of primary tumor
Carcinoma in situ (intraductal tumor)
Solitary tumor without vascular invasion
Solitary tumor ≤5 cm without vascular invasion
Solitary tumor >5 cm without vascular invasion
Solitary tumor with intrahepatic vascular invasion or multiple tumors, with or without vascular invasion
Tumor perforating the visceral peritoneum
Tumor involving local extrahepatic structures by direct invasion

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ.
Solitary tumor ≤5 cm without vascular invasion.
Solitary tumor >5 cm without vascular invasion.
Solitary tumor with vascular invasion or multiple tumors.
Tumor perforates visceral peritoneum.

stage_groups.criteria: T4 N0 M0 or Any T N1 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Direct invasion of extrahepatic structures or regional lymph node metastasis.
Distant metastasis.

staging_pearls: T1 is divided by tumor size (≤5 cm vs >5 cm) without vascular invasion [19].
T2 includes both solitary tumor with vascular invasion and multiple tumors (with or without vascular invasion).
Regional lymph nodes include those associated with the hilar hepatic artery, portal vein, and cystic duct, among others [INTRA-C, b].

extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_clinical.typical_presentation: Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) typically presents with jaundice followed by evidence of biliary obstruction or abnormality on imaging. Pain may be present, and liver function tests are often abnormal. Tumors are located in the biliary tree from the hilum to the ampulla of Vater [EXTRA-1].

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Painless obstructive jaundice is the most common presentation.

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain.

signs.name: Jaundice
signs.detail: Icterus visible on sclera and skin.

signs.name: Courvoisier sign
signs.detail: Palpable, nontender gallbladder in the setting of obstructive jaundice.

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage disease may be asymptomatic or present with vague symptoms.
Cholangitis (fever, jaundice, abdominal pain) may be the initial presentation.
May be misdiagnosed as primary sclerosing cholangitis or IgG4-related cholangitis [97-100].

extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_red_flag: Cholangitis (Charcot's triad: fever, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain)

diagnostic.test: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI with IV contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Essential for assessing tumor extent, vascular invasion, and resectability [EXTRA-1, BIL-A].
diagnostic.key_findings: Biliary duct dilation, mass, vascular involvement.

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred noninvasive cholangiography to evaluate biliary anatomy and obstruction [EXTRA-1, d].

diagnostic.test: Chest CT with or without contrast

diagnostic.rationale: Primarily therapeutic (biliary drainage/stenting) rather than diagnostic; may obtain brush cytology [EXTRA-1, d].
diagnostic.key_findings: Therapeutic stent placement; cytology may show malignant cells.

diagnostic.rationale: May help define a mass or abnormal thickening and guide biopsy, especially for distal tumors [EXTRA-1].
Required for diagnosis before systemic therapy; consider after determining transplant candidacy [EXTRA-1, h].

staging.rationale: May identify occult peritoneal metastases; yield is variable (14-45%) [EXTRA-1, 214].
staging.indication: Consider in patients with potentially resectable disease.

biomarkers.rationale: Baseline tumor markers; not for diagnosis [EXTRA-1, e].
Assess biliary obstruction and hepatic function [EXTRA-1].
To rule out IgG4-related cholangitis, which can mimic eCCA [EXTRA-1, f].

do_not_miss: High-quality biliary protocol imaging (preferably CT) prior to stenting to evaluate tumor extent [BIL-A].
Core biopsy is preferred over FNA for tissue diagnosis [EXTRA-1, h].
Multidisciplinary evaluation including hepatobiliary surgeon, radiologist, and oncologist.
avoid: Avoid transperitoneal biopsy in potential transplant candidates as it may preclude transplantation [EXTRA-1, h].
Avoid unnecessary biliary drainage before imaging evaluation in resectable disease.

extrahepatic_cholangiocarcinoma_staging.system: AJCC 8th edition (2017) – separate staging for perihilar (hilar) and distal bile duct tumors

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed
No evidence of primary tumor
Carcinoma in situ/high-grade dysplasia
Tumor confined to the bile duct, with extension up to the muscle layer or fibrous tissue (perihilar) OR Tumor invades the bile duct wall with depth less than 5 mm (distal)

Tumor invades beyond the wall of the bile duct to surrounding adipose tissue, or tumor invades adjacent hepatic parenchyma (perihilar) OR Tumor invades the bile duct wall with depth of 5–12 mm (distal)
Tumor invades beyond the wall of the bile duct to surrounding adipose tissue (perihilar)
Tumor invades adjacent hepatic parenchyma (perihilar)
Tumor invades unilateral branches of the portal vein or hepatic artery (perihilar) OR Tumor invades the bile duct wall with depth greater than 12 mm (distal)

Tumor invades main portal vein or its branches bilaterally, or the common hepatic artery; or unilateral second-order biliary radicals bilaterally with contralateral portal vein or hepatic artery involvement (perihilar) OR Tumor involves the celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and/or common hepatic artery (distal)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

Metastasis in one to three regional lymph nodes (distal) OR N1 for perihilar: one to three positive lymph nodes typically involving the hilar, cystic duct, common bile duct, hepatic artery, posterior pancreatoduodenal, and portal vein lymph nodes
Metastasis in four or more regional lymph nodes (distal) OR N2 for perihilar: four or more positive lymph nodes from the sites described for N1

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ.
Tumor confined to bile duct wall.

stage_groups.criteria: T1 N1 M0 or T2 N0 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Early nodal disease or local invasion beyond bile duct.

stage_groups.criteria: T2 N1 M0 or T3 N0-1 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: More advanced local disease or nodal involvement.
Extensive regional nodal disease.
Locally advanced disease with major vessel or biliary involvement.
Distant metastasis.

staging_pearls: Perihilar (hilar) and distal eCCA have separate staging definitions in AJCC 8th edition [19, ST-3, ST-4].
For perihilar tumors, T stage is based on extent of bile duct involvement, hepatic parenchyma invasion, and vascular involvement.
For distal tumors, T stage is based on depth of invasion into the bile duct wall (T1: <5 mm, T2: 5-12 mm, T3: >12 mm) [19].
Regional lymph node staging differs between perihilar (N1: 1-3 nodes, N2: ≥4 nodes) and distal (N1: 1-3 nodes, N2: ≥4 nodes) but with different nodal groups.

management_principles.overview: Biliary tract cancers (BTCs) are highly lethal malignancies requiring a multidisciplinary approach. Treatment philosophy emphasizes complete surgical resection as the only potentially curative option for resectable disease, with multimodal therapy (systemic therapy, locoregional therapy, radiation) integrated for advanced stages. The approach is guided by tumor subsite (gallbladder, intrahepatic, extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma), resectability status, and presence of metastases. Clinical trial participation is encouraged for all stages [1].

by_intent.approach: Surgery is the primary treatment with intent to achieve negative margins. Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or chemoradiation is recommended for node-positive or margin-positive disease. For locally advanced unresectable disease, neoadjuvant systemic therapy or clinical trial participation is considered to evaluate tumor biology and avoid futile surgery.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with unresectable or metastatic disease
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (preferred: gemcitabine/cisplatin + immunotherapy) is the mainstay. Locoregional therapies (ablation, TACE, Y90) and radiation may be considered for symptom control or oligometastatic disease. Best supportive care is an option for poor performance status.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary team (MDT) evaluation is mandatory for all patients with BTCs to determine resectability, optimize staging, and guide treatment decisions. MDT should include hepatobiliary surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, radiologists, pathologists, gastroenterologists, and palliative care specialists. For patients with hilar cholangiocarcinoma, consultation with a transplant center is recommended for select cases [2].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a key determinant of treatment tolerance. Patients with ECOG PS 0-1 are candidates for all standard therapies. For PS 2, reduced-intensity regimens or clinical trials are appropriate. PS ≥3 indicates best supportive care. Biliary drainage should be considered for symptomatic jaundice prior to systemic therapy to improve PS and allow treatment [3].

management_pathways.title: Incidental Finding of Gallbladder Cancer During Surgery

nodes.criteria: Incidental suspicious mass during surgery, hepatobiliary expertise available

options.label: Intraoperative staging ± biopsy
options.recommendation: Perform cholecystectomy + en bloc hepatic resection + lymphadenectomy ± bile duct excision for malignant involvement.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (GALL-6).

nodes.criteria: Incidental suspicious mass, expertise unavailable OR resectability unclear

options.label: Document findings, refer to hepatobiliary surgeon
options.recommendation: Do not perform biopsy (risk of peritoneal dissemination). Proceed with staging workup.

management_pathways.title: Incidental Finding of Gallbladder Cancer on Pathologic Review

options.label: Observe
options.recommendation: No further treatment; follow surveillance guidelines.

nodes.criteria: T1b or greater, OR T1a with positive margins, OR cystic duct node positive

options.label: Hepatic resection + lymphadenectomy ± bile duct excision
options.recommendation: If resectable, proceed to surgery. Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for locoregionally advanced disease to rule out rapid progression.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (GALL-6).

management_pathways.title: Gallbladder Cancer Presenting as Mass on Imaging

options.label: Cholecystectomy + en bloc hepatic resection + lymphadenectomy ± bile duct excision
options.recommendation: Diagnostic laparoscopy recommended prior to definitive resection. Adjuvant treatment per post-resection status (GALL-6).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (GALL-6).

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred) OR clinical trial (preferred) OR palliative radiation therapy
options.recommendation: Biomarker testing recommended. Assess for response; consider re-evaluation for resection or locoregional therapy.

management_pathways.title: Gallbladder Cancer with Jaundice and/or Metastatic Disease

options.label: Cholecystectomy + en bloc hepatic resection + lymphadenectomy + bile duct excision
options.recommendation: Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B) to rule out rapid progression. Jaundice is a relative contraindication.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (GALL-6).

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred) OR clinical trial (preferred) OR best supportive care
options.recommendation: Biomarker testing recommended. Consider biliary drainage if indicated. For palliative radiation, see Principles of Radiation Therapy.

options.label: Resection + regional lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Consider ablation for small tumors (<3 cm). Staging laparoscopy may be considered.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (INTRA-2).

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred) OR clinical trial (preferred) OR locoregional therapy (arterially directed therapies or RT)
options.recommendation: Consider combination of chemotherapy and chemoradiation or chemoradiation alone. For select patients, referral to transplant center may be considered.

management_pathways.title: Extrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA, including hilar and distal)

options.label: Resection (pancreaticoduodenectomy for distal, major hepatic resection for hilar) + lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: Biliary drainage may be considered preoperatively. Frozen section assessment of duct margins recommended.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (preferred: capecitabine) or observation per post-resection status (EXTRA-2).

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred) OR clinical trial (preferred) OR combination of chemotherapy and chemoradiation OR chemoradiation OR palliative RT
options.recommendation: Biopsy recommended after determining transplant status. Biliary drainage if indicated. Consider referral to transplant center for select cases.

items.recommendation: Required for all patients
ECOG or Karnofsky score
Critical for surgical planning
If present, consider biliary drainage

items.text: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast
items.recommendation: Preferred for staging; assess vascular invasion, lymph nodes, metastases
For pulmonary metastasis evaluation
For extrahepatic CCA; preferably CT/MRI before biliary decompression
For biliary anatomy assessment; ERCP/PTC reserved for therapeutic intervention
For gallbladder cancer and iCCA to detect occult peritoneal disease
Not routine; may be considered for equivocal findings or on case-by-case basis
Baseline assessment
Baseline tests, not for diagnosis; CA 19-9 may be falsely elevated in jaundice
Baseline
Per ASCO guidelines if indicated
If HCC cannot be excluded

items.text: Core biopsy is preferred over FNA
items.recommendation: For tissue diagnosis and biomarker testing
Recommended for unresectable/metastatic BTC candidates for systemic therapy; includes NTRK, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, BRAF V600E, FGFR2, IDH1, HER2, RET, KRAS G12C, NRG1 fusions
For patients with young age, strong family history, or no known risk factors for liver disease
Early, before biliary drainage if possible
For systemic therapy planning
If RT considered
For endoscopic procedures
For select hilar CCA or iCCA patients
If dMMR/MSI-H or family history suggestive of BRCA1/2 PVs

surgery.role: Surgery is the only potentially curative treatment for resectable BTCs. Complete resection with negative margins (R0) is the goal.
principles: Resectability must be assessed by high-quality cross-sectional imaging and MDT review.
Preoperative biopsy is not always necessary if clinical suspicion is high, but should be performed if chemotherapy is anticipated.
Diagnostic laparoscopy should be considered to rule out occult metastatic disease, especially for gallbladder cancer and iCCA.
Regional lymphadenectomy is recommended for accurate staging (porta hepatis for hilar, head of pancreas area for distal).
Minimally invasive approaches are safe and effective for well-selected cases by experienced surgeons.
Resection of the bile duct is only indicated to obtain negative margins, not routinely.
Port site resection is not recommended as it does not improve outcomes.

procedures.name: Cholecystectomy + en bloc hepatic resection (segments IVB and V) + lymphadenectomy
procedures.operative_mortality: Varies by center; generally <5% in high-volume centers

procedures.name: Major hepatectomy (right or left) with caudate lobectomy + hilar lymphadenectomy ± portal vein resection
procedures.key_complications: Liver failure, biliary leak, vascular injury

procedures.name: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure)
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic leak, delayed gastric emptying, hemorrhage

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by the ability to achieve an R0 resection while maintaining adequate liver function (future liver remnant ≥30-40%). Contraindications to resection include: distant metastatic disease (liver, peritoneum), lymph node metastases beyond the regional basin (celiac axis, aortocaval groove for gallbladder), extensive vascular involvement precluding safe resection, and contralateral biliary/vascular involvement (for hilar CCA). For hilar CCA, the Blumgart staging system may predict resectability.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Preoperative biliary drainage should be considered for patients with jaundice prior to resection and systemic therapy, especially for hilar CCA with a small future liver remnant. Portal vein embolization may be considered to hypertrophy the future liver remnant. Nutritional optimization and correction of coagulopathy are essential.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is used in the adjuvant setting for resected disease with positive margins or node-positive disease, for unresectable disease in combination with chemotherapy, and for palliation of symptomatic primary or metastatic lesions.
principles: RT dosing is based on the ability to meet normal organ constraints and underlying liver function.
Image-guided RT (IGRT) is strongly recommended when using RT, IMRT, and SBRT to improve accuracy and reduce toxicity.
Concurrent chemoradiation should be limited to 5-fluorouracil or capecitabine; concurrent gemcitabine is not recommended due to toxicity.
RT can be considered for all tumor locations if organ constraints are met.
Brachytherapy may be considered at experienced centers.

dose_frameworks.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)
dose_frameworks.indication: Unresectable intrahepatic or extrahepatic CCA; BED10 >100 Gy preferred if constraints allow

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionated RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Unresectable disease at centers with experience; photons or protons

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional Fractionation RT
dose_frameworks.indication: If SBRT/hypofractionation not feasible; may be combined with chemotherapy

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45 Gy to regional nodes; 50-60 Gy to tumor bed
dose_frameworks.indication: Resected eCCA or gallbladder cancer with positive margins or high-risk features

approaches.name: Concurrent Chemoradiation (Capecitabine or 5-FU)
approaches.indication: Unresectable disease; adjuvant setting for high-risk features

approaches.name: Palliative Radiation Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Various short courses (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions)
approaches.indication: Symptom control for primary tumor, bone metastases, brain metastases

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is the primary treatment for unresectable or metastatic BTCs. First-line standard is gemcitabine/cisplatin with or without immunotherapy (durvalumab or pembrolizumab). Second-line options include FOLFOX, FOLFIRI, liposomal irinotecan combinations, and targeted therapies based on biomarker testing. Adjuvant capecitabine is recommended for resected disease.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: BILCAP trial showed improved overall survival in per-protocol analysis.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, single-agent gemcitabine or fluorouracil/leucovorin

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Regimens used for unresectable/metastatic disease (see below)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: To evaluate tumor biology and avoid futile surgery.
TOPAZ-1 and KEYNOTE-966 trials showed improved OS.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/gemcitabine (category 1), albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, capecitabine/oxaliplatin, FOLFOX, gemcitabine/oxaliplatin, single agents
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None, but biomarker testing recommended for potential targeted therapy

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ABC-06 trial showed OS benefit vs. active symptom control.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: FOLFIRI, fluorouracil/leucovorin/liposomal irinotecan (category 2B), regorafenib (category 2B), or regimens used in first-line if not previously used
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Biomarker testing should guide targeted therapy options

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Biomarker-directed therapies have demonstrated efficacy in specific subsets.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Durvalumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, myelosuppression, fatigue, hepatotoxicity, immune-related adverse events

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nausea, myelosuppression, fatigue, immune-related adverse events

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 bolus then 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours

key_regimens.name: Futibatinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hyperphosphatemia, stomatitis, dry eye, nail toxicity

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fever, fatigue, nausea, rash, cardiac toxicity

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment typically performed every 2-3 cycles during systemic therapy or as clinically indicated. Post-treatment surveillance follows guidelines based on recurrence risk.
response_logic: Assessment of treatment response should be performed using RECIST criteria via cross-sectional imaging.
After primary treatment (surgery or systemic therapy), resectability or locoregional therapy should be reconsidered if response is observed.
For patients with progression on systemic therapy, subsequent-line therapy should be considered based on biomarkers, prior treatment, and performance status.
For patients with localized disease post-resection, surveillance should include periodic imaging and tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA) as clinically indicated.
imaging_recommendations: During systemic therapy: Multiphasic abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI with IV contrast and chest CT ± contrast every 2-3 months.
Post-resection surveillance: Consider imaging every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for up to 5 years, or as clinically indicated.
For gallbladder cancer: Follow BILCAP trial surveillance schedule (every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for up to 5 years).
PET/CT may be considered for equivocal findings.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of progressing lesions may be warranted to reassess histology or obtain biomarker testing for targeted therapy.
For patients with recurrent disease after resection, re-evaluation by MDT is essential to determine eligibility for further surgery, locoregional therapy, or systemic therapy.
Salvage options include second-line systemic therapy, clinical trials, or best supportive care.
